past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 5 years ago, on warfarin but self-discontinued 3 weeks ago due to nosebleeds
Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, poorly controlled
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago
Congestive heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), NYHA class II
History of deep vein thrombosis in left leg, 7 years ago
family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 71, fatal
Father had hypertension and died of renal failure at age 74
Sister with type 2 diabetes and atrial fibrillation
Brother had myocardial infarction at age 62

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a second-floor apartment; daughter lives nearby
social_history.exercise: Walks to church twice weekly, otherwise sedentary

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, moderately distressed. Oriented to person and place but not to date. Severe expressive aphasia with nonfluent, effortful speech; able to say her name and 'yes/no' inconsistently. Comprehension partially preserved for simple commands. Unable to name, repeat, read, or write. Attention difficult to assess given aphasia.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal at 3 mm, briskly reactive. Conjugate right gaze deviation with forced gaze preference; able to cross midline briefly with oculocephalic maneuver. Right homonymous hemianopia to confrontation (blink-to-threat testing). Right lower facial droop, forehead sparing. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue protrudes to the right.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: flaccid, 0/5 strength in deltoid, biceps, wrist extensors, and grip. Right lower extremity: 1/5 hip flexion, 0/5 ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: full strength 5/5 throughout. Right arm drifts immediately to bed on pronator drift testing.
neurological_exam.sensory: Absent response to pinprick and light touch in right face, arm, and leg. Right-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. Proprioception absent in right hand and foot.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+. Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+ with extensor plantar response (positive Babinski sign on the right). Left plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess on the right due to plegia. Left finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact without dysmetria.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate due to right hemiplegia.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 19. Right hemineglect present on visual field testing. No meningeal signs. No nystagmus.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Jing Li is a 78-year-old right-handed African American woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (recently self-discontinued warfarin three weeks ago due to recurrent epistaxis), poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction who was brought to the emergency department by EMS after an acute onset of right-sided weakness and speech difficulty. Her daughter reports that she spoke with her mother on the phone at approximately 09:00 this morning and she was completely normal, discussing plans for a family dinner this weekend. At approximately 10:45, a neighbor found Mrs. Thompson on the floor of her kitchen, awake but unable to speak coherently and with her right arm and leg limp. The neighbor called 911 immediately. EMS arrived at 10:58 and documented right gaze preference, dense right hemiplegia, and expressive aphasia. A stroke alert was activated in the field. Prehospital finger-stick glucose was 204 mg/dL. The patient was transported emergently to the nearest comprehensive stroke center, arriving at 11:22. On arrival, telemetry immediately demonstrated an irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 108 bpm. The patient was visibly anxious and attempting to speak but producing only fragmentary, effortful words. She was able to follow simple one-step commands with her left hand. Her daughter arrived shortly after and confirmed that her mother had stopped taking warfarin approximately three weeks ago because she was having frequent nosebleeds and was afraid of 'bleeding out.' She had not yet seen her cardiologist to discuss alternative anticoagulation. The daughter also notes her mother's blood pressure has been running high at home despite medications, with readings in the 170s-180s systolic. There was no preceding headache, seizure, recent trauma, fever, or change in baseline function prior to today's event. Last known well time is established at 09:00, approximately 2 hours and 22 minutes prior to ED arrival.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery territory involving the left frontal lobe (including Broca's area), left parietal lobe, and left insular cortex
findings.size: Approximately 4.8 x 3.5 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Markedly hyperintense signal indicating restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the affected territory suggesting onset greater than 3 hours
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in the left MCA territory
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal corresponding to the area of infarction

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement in the left frontoparietal region. No midline shift. Mild effacement of the left lateral ventricle frontal horn.
findings.borders: Well-demarcated margins conforming to the left MCA superior division vascular territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: Abrupt cutoff of the left MCA at the proximal M1 segment with absent distal flow
signal_characteristics.TOF: Loss of flow-related enhancement in the left MCA distal to the M1 segment

additional_observations: Right MCA, bilateral ACA, and bilateral PCA are patent on MRA
Mild to moderate generalized cerebral volume loss
mri.impression: Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion involving the left frontal lobe (including Broca's area), parietal lobe, and insular cortex. No hemorrhagic transformation. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Cornell criteria met)
ST depression in leads V4-V6 likely secondary to LVH (strain pattern)
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 108 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy with repolarization abnormality (strain pattern). Left atrial abnormality. No acute ischemic ST changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 11.3 x10^9/L (H), MCV 79.3 fL (L), Chloride 94 mEq/L (L), BUN 24 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (H), Glucose 205 mg/dL (H), Total Cholesterol 219 mg/dL (H), LDL 136 mg/dL (H), HDL 42 mg/dL (L), Triglycerides 171 mg/dL (H), Troponin I 0.05 ng/mL (H), HbA1c 8.1 % (H), BNP 404 pg/mL (H).

findings.description: CTA: Complete occlusion of the left MCA at the M1 segment with absent distal opacification. Left ICA terminus is patent. Thrombus appears to originate at the M1 origin.

findings.location: Bilateral carotid bifurcations and aortic arch
findings.description: CTA: Mild calcified plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations with less than 30% stenosis bilaterally. Atherosclerotic changes of the aortic arch. No flow-limiting stenosis of the vertebral or basilar arteries.

additional_observations: Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with normal caliber
Basilar artery is patent without stenosis
Circle of Willis is complete with bilateral A1 and P1 segments present
No evidence of dissection in cervical or intracranial vessels
output.impression: Mild bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerosis without hemodynamically significant stenosis. No cervical or intracranial dissection. Clinical correlation recommended.

chambers.Left Atrial Appendage: Poorly visualized on TTE; cannot exclude thrombus
chambers.Right Ventricle: Normal size and function, TAPSE 2.1 cm

findings: Diastolic Function: Grade II diastolic dysfunction
output.impression: Abnormal values: Left Atrial Volume Index 42 mL/m2 (H), Left Ventricular Wall Thickness Concentric LVH, septal wall 1.4 cm  (H), Diastolic Function Grade II diastolic dysfunction  (H), Mitral Valve Moderate mitral regurgitation  (H), Estimated RVSP 38 mmHg  (H).

events.description: No periods of sinus rhythm detected
Episodes of rapid ventricular response (>120 bpm) totaling 3.2 hours, predominantly during daytime hours
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (68 in 24 hours, <1% burden)

findings: No periods of sinus rhythm detected
Episodes of rapid ventricular response (>120 bpm) totaling 3.2 hours, predominantly during daytime hours
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (68 in 24 hours, <1% burden)
output.impression: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals persistent atrial fibrillation with suboptimal rate control (average 92 bpm with frequent episodes of rapid ventricular response). No significant pauses or ventricular arrhythmias.

findings.region: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
findings.signal: Peak_systolic_velocity: 98 cm/s; End_diastolic_velocity: 32 cm/s; ICA_CCA_ratio: 1.5; Estimated_stenosis: Less than 50% (NASCET criteria)

findings.region: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
findings.signal: Peak_systolic_velocity: 88 cm/s; End_diastolic_velocity: 28 cm/s; ICA_CCA_ratio: 1.3; Estimated_stenosis: Less than 50% (NASCET criteria)

output.impression: Mild bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerotic disease, less than 50% stenosis bilaterally. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. The absence of significant carotid stenosis supports a cardioembolic mechanism rather than large artery atherosclerosis as the primary stroke etiology. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: acute ischemic stroke atrial fibrillation anticoagulation timing guidelines

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: For patients with AF-related stroke, initiation of anticoagulation should be individualized based on infarct size and hemorrhagic transformation risk. Generally, 4-14 days post-stroke depending on severity.

results.title: Timing of Anticoagulation After Acute Ischaemic Stroke (ELAN Trial)
results.authors: Fischer U, Koga M, et al.
results.key_finding: Early anticoagulation (within 48 hours for minor/moderate strokes) with DOACs was non-inferior to delayed anticoagulation and may reduce recurrent ischemic events without increasing bleeding risk.

results.title: European Stroke Organisation guidelines on secondary prevention after TIA and ischaemic stroke
results.key_finding: DOACs are preferred over warfarin for AF-related stroke prevention. CHA2DS2-VASc score should guide anticoagulation decisions.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend initiating anticoagulation with a DOAC (e.g., apixaban, rivarelbanan) for secondary stroke prevention in patients with AF-related ischemic stroke. Timing depends on infarct severity: early initiation (48 hours) may be appropriate for small-to-moderate strokes, while larger infarctions should wait 7-14 days. DOACs are preferred over warfarin due to lower intracranial hemorrhage risk. The patient's CHA2DS2-VASc score should be calculated; this patient scores highly (age, female sex, hypertension, diabetes, heart failure, prior stroke) indicating high annual stroke risk without anticoagulation.

interactions: Glipizide: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction, but combined use may increase bleeding risk if hypoglycemia causes falls
warnings: Renal function: CrCl estimated at 48 mL/min; standard dosing appropriate (dose reduction criteria: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, or creatinine >=1.5 mg/dL - patient meets one criterion but needs two of three for dose reduction)
Monitor renal function periodically given CKD stage 3a
No concomitant strong CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors identified in current medication list
alternatives: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily with evening meal (renal dose adjustment at CrCl 15-50: 15 mg daily)
Edoxaban 60 mg daily (dose reduce to 30 mg if CrCl 15-50 or weight <=60 kg)
Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily (consider 110 mg BID if age >80 or CrCl 30-50)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left middle cerebral artery territory, cardioembolic etiology secondary to atrial fibrillation

differential.key_distinguishing: CT and MRI show no intracranial hemorrhage; DWI restriction in a vascular territory confirms ischemic etiology
No witnessed seizure activity, no seizure history, persistent deficits, and imaging confirms acute infarction
Age 68 with no migraine history, persistent deficits with imaging confirmation of infarction
Prehospital glucose was 204 mg/dL and ED glucose 218 mg/dL; hypoglycemia is excluded
MRI shows vascular territory DWI restriction without enhancing mass; abrupt onset pattern inconsistent with tumor

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid assessment including last known well time, NIHSS scoring, and fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 19, LKW approximately 2h22m prior, glucose 204 mg/dL

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude intracranial hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage, possible early ischemic changes in left MCA territory

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat laboratories including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, troponin, and glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation confirming patient is not anticoagulated, hyperglycemia, mildly elevated creatinine
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, confirming cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Administer IV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% as bolus with remainder infused over 60 minutes, within the 4.5-hour treatment window
Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion and thrombectomy candidacy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left MCA M1 occlusion, mild bilateral carotid atherosclerosis

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurointerventional surgery for emergent mechanical thrombectomy given LVO
Manage blood pressure to target <185/110 pre-tPA and <180/105 for 24 hours post-tPA
Obtain brain MRI with DWI to define infarct extent and assess for hemorrhagic transformation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left MCA territory infarction with DWI restriction

optimal_actions.action: Admit to neurological ICU or dedicated stroke unit for monitoring with serial neurological assessments
Initiate anticoagulation with a DOAC (apixaban 5 mg BID) for secondary stroke prevention once hemorrhagic transformation excluded, approximately 7-14 days post-stroke given large infarct size
Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure and function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved EF with LVH, dilated left atrium, diastolic dysfunction consistent with HFpEF and chronic AF

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility
Obtain emergent non-contrast CT to exclude hemorrhage before administering thrombolytics
Administer IV alteplase within the 4.5-hour treatment window
Identify large vessel occlusion and arrange mechanical thrombectomy
Obtain ECG to confirm atrial fibrillation as the stroke mechanism
Confirm patient is not currently anticoagulated (INR normal) before tPA administration
Initiate anticoagulation with DOAC for secondary stroke prevention once acute phase is stable
contraindicated_actions: Administering anticoagulation (heparin, DOAC) in the hyperacute phase before excluding hemorrhagic transformation
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute setting (risk of worsening penumbral ischemia)
Giving antiplatelet agents concurrently with IV alteplase within 24 hours
Delaying IV alteplase to wait for MRI in a patient within the treatment window with no CT contraindication
Restarting warfarin rather than transitioning to a DOAC given its association with the epistaxis that led to non-adherence
Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of acute large territory stroke
key_reasoning_points: Sudden onset right hemiplegia and nonfluent aphasia in a patient with known AF and recent self-discontinuation of anticoagulation is a classic presentation of cardioembolic ischemic stroke
The self-discontinuation of warfarin three weeks ago due to epistaxis is the proximate cause; the INR of 1.1 confirms she is no longer therapeutically anticoagulated
ECG showing AF with RVR confirms the cardioembolic source and explains the stroke mechanism
MRI with DWI hyperintensity and ADC hypointensity in the left MCA territory with MRA showing M1 occlusion confirms large vessel occlusion ischemic stroke
Normal INR makes the patient eligible for IV alteplase; the approximately 2.5-hour window from last known well is within the 4.5-hour treatment threshold
LVO mandates consideration of mechanical thrombectomy in addition to IV thrombolysis (bridging therapy)
Multiple uncontrolled risk factors (hypertension, diabetes with HbA1c 8.4%, hyperlipidemia) compound stroke risk and require aggressive secondary prevention
DOAC (apixaban) is preferred over warfarin for long-term anticoagulation given better safety profile and the patient's prior intolerance of warfarin side effects

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - sudden onset right hemiplegia and aphasia in a patient with known AF who self-discontinued anticoagulation, with clear LVO on imaging
clinical_pearls: Self-discontinuation of anticoagulation is a common real-world scenario leading to cardioembolic stroke; always inquire about medication adherence
CHA2DS2-VASc score in this patient is 7 (female, age 65-74, HTN, DM, CHF, vascular disease, prior stroke), predicting very high annual stroke risk without anticoagulation
DOACs are preferred over warfarin for AF-related stroke prevention due to lower ICH risk and no INR monitoring requirement, which may improve adherence
Poorly controlled hypertension contributes to both small vessel disease and increases hemorrhagic transformation risk after thrombolysis
